title: podman pull does not use https_proxy when using podman machine, but will use on linux
body:
### Issue Description

Hello. I'm making a mitm proxy that prevents image downloading when the critical vulnerability was found.
I noticed that the behavior for reading environment variables on macos is very different from the behavior on linux.
I would like to be able to specify a proxy only for single image pulling command without exporting it globally, as in docker (just like how podman works on linux right now).

I read this discussion, but I need to be able to launch the podman machine without a proxy (because it will not start if I try to get podman/machine-os through it, since the proxy is not ready yet), and only then use it for pulling:
https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/11941



### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. podman machine init
2. podman machine start
3. https_proxy="https://user:pass@127.0.0.1:9991" podman pull alpine:3.18


### Describe the results you received

linux: podman used the proxy
macos: podman didn't use the proxy

### Describe the results you expected

linux: podman used the proxy
macos: podman used the proxy

### podman info output

comments.body: does it work if the environment variable is set prior to the command (like with export) ? i guess you are saying yes and no.  No because you dont want to use the proxy for the  machine image pull or general machine communications.  You only want to use the proxy for pulling a specific image.
temporary workaround would be to `podman machine ssh` and then issue your pull?

Hello, thanks for response.

> Does it work if the environment variable is set prior to the command (like with export)? 
I guess you are saying yes and no. 

Yes and no. It won't work if variable is set before pull command, it could work for machine init, but then its not suitable, cause it requires restarting the podman machine to switch.

> Temporary workaround would be to podman machine ssh and then issue your pull?

Yes, but only as a temporary. I think the proxy can be changed inside the machine, but I don’t know the specific config to make the change.

> Yes, but only as a temporary. I think the proxy can be changed inside the machine, but I don’t know the specific config to make the change.

I could use something like this:
```
podman machine ssh
~/.config/containers/containers.conf
env = ["https_proxy=https://user:pass@127.0.0.1:9991"]
```

But its read-only fs.
Exported variables will be erased after leaving the session.
Pulling from the inside is tricky since I want to access host machine.

Setting in a conf file will still keep it for all other commands so that isn't what you asked initially, if you just want to have it on a single pull this should work
```
podman machine ssh https_proxy="https://user:pass@127.0.0.1:9991/" podman pull alpine:3.18
```

> If you just want to have it on a single pull this should work: podman machine ssh [cmd].

Hello. As I said earlier, pulling from the inside is tricky since I want to access host machine (it will pass the variable, but connection will be refused since it localhost).

You always pull from the inside even if you issue podman pull on macos, you containers are run in the VM thus all images are pulled in the VM doesn't matter where the command was issues which is precisely why the server uses the proxy env from the VM and cannot just switch to the one from the client.

If you want to connect to localhost from within the machine use host.containers.internal as hostname instead of localhost, the default proxy env logic on machine start will automatically replace that for you.

> The default proxy env logic on machine start will automatically replace that for you.

Thanks for the host.containers.internal suggestion. Not sure about automatic replacement, but podman reached the proxy when it was specified manually as far as we have been able to test.

Now he needs to add the necessary ca to work. As I understand it, it’s enough to add them to /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors, following this tutorial?
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/tutorials/podman-install-certificate-authority.md

> Now he needs to add the necessary ca to work. As I understand it, it’s enough to add them to /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors, following this tutorial?

It will not work because cert is signed for 127.0.0.1, not host.containers.internal...
I wouldn't want to insert an extra SAN when signing proxy's cert for just this.

```
tls: failed to verify certificate: x509: certificate is not valid for any names, but wanted to match host.containers.internal
```
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
